title: Cheese scones
subtitle: Savoury scones with mature cheddar and a high, crumbly rise
excerpt: Savoury scones made with self-raising flour and mature cheddar throughout the dough, with extra cheese scattered on top before baking. The same light-hand technique as fruit scones applies. Good with butter only, or split and filled with ham. The cheese on top forms a golden, slightly crisp crust as it bakes.
sourceNotes: Savoury cheese scones are a British variant of the standard scone recipe, with grated mature cheddar mixed into the dough and scattered on top. They appear in British domestic baking books from at least the mid-20th century, when cheddar became widely available as a staple. The recipe uses the same method as a plain scone (rubbed-in fat, self-raising flour, buttermilk liquid) with the cheese folded in before the liquid is added. They are associated with pub lunches, farmhouse teas, and as an accompaniment to soup.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked scones on the day of baking. Defrost at room temperature and warm in a 160°C oven for 5 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten on the day. The dough can be cut, frozen unbaked, and baked from frozen at 220°C for 18 to 20 minutes.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 200°C. The cheese on top chars slightly at this temperature, which is correct. A lower temperature gives a pale, less flavourful topping.

recipeTools.notes: one baking tray, lined with baking paper

glossaryTerms.definition: The characteristic topping of a cheese scone: cheese scattered on top before baking melts and crisps in the oven heat, forming a golden, slightly puffed crust separate from the main scone body. The extra cheese on top concentrates the flavour and adds textural contrast to the soft interior. Using mature cheddar in both the dough and the topping (rather than a milder cheese) gives a pronounced flavour. The scone must be cut to a height of at least 2.5 cm so the top crust stays in proportion to the body.

content.text: The same light-touch principle applies as for fruit scones. Cheese scones benefit from a slightly deeper cut than sweet scones (at least 2.5 cm) so the
stays in proportion to the rise. Use the full 100 g of cheese throughout the recipe: 75 g into the dough and 25 g on top. Do not use mild cheddar here; the flavour all but disappears in baking.

Heat the oven to 220°C conventional (200°C fan) and line a baking tray with baking paper. Sift the flour, baking powder, and salt into a large bowl. Rub in the cold butter until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. Stir in the 75 g grated cheddar. Beat the egg with the buttermilk and pour into the flour mixture. Stir with a round-bladed knife until the dough just comes together.

Turn onto a lightly floured surface and press out to 2.5 cm thickness. Cut into rounds using a 6 cm cutter, pressing straight down without twisting. Place on the tray, brush the tops with a little beaten egg or milk, and scatter the remaining 25 g cheddar over the tops. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until well risen and golden, with the cheese on top bubbling and crisping. Transfer to a rack. Eat warm with cold butter.
